context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the answers for 6.1 rn tbh, esp the first 3 modes + frequencies.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the answers directly — that's not how we work here! But let's get you to figure it out yourself, which won't take long if you think it through.

Start here: what are the boundary conditions at each end of the pipe? What does the displacement ψ have to do at the closed end, and at the open end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct answers, which I must refuse per the rules. I should redirect them to think about the physics themselves using the Socratic method. I'll start with a foundational question about boundary conditions to get them thinking about what determines the normal modes.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm short on time lol, can u at least just tell me the boundary conditions for ψ and pressure so i can write it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the answers isn't my role — and honestly, this will go faster than you think.

One quick question: physically, can air move at a closed wall? What does that tell you about ψ there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for direct answers, now framing it as a time constraint. I need to hold firm on my role and not give the boundary conditions directly. I should be clear about my role and redirect them to think about the physics. A single pointed question should be enough to get them started quickly.

exchanges.student: nope, so ψ(0)=0 i think. can u just do the open end one + give the mode formula?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the closed end. Now same logic for the open end: at an open end, what physical quantity is fixed (think pressure), and what does that imply for ψ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the closed end boundary condition correct. I should acknowledge that briefly and then push them to reason about the open end themselves with a targeted question. Still refusing to give the mode formula directly.

exchanges.student: idk rn, just gimme the relation between pressure and ψ pls so i can finish it myself "technically".
exchanges.tutor: That's still giving you the answer in disguise — I can't do that. But think about it physically: at an open end, the pipe meets the atmosphere. What happens to the pressure there compared to atmospheric pressure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to provide key pieces of the solution by framing it as just a 'relation.' I should not give this directly. Instead, I should ask a guiding question that helps them recall the physics from their course materials. A hint toward their textbook or lecture notes is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: atm pressure so pressure variation is zero at x=L, got it. can u at least confirm if that means antinode for ψ and nodes for pressure?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's correct! So now you have both boundary conditions for ψ: a node at x=0 and an antinode at x=L. Given that, can you sketch what the first normal mode looks like over the length L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned correctly about the pressure and displacement relationship at the open end. I should confirm this is correct and now push them to draw the modes themselves, moving the conversation forward toward the actual solution rather than lingering on boundary conditions.

exchanges.student: yeah half a quarter-wave i think 😭 can u just list the allowed k values for closed-open so i don't mess up the frequencies?
exchanges.tutor: Almost — but 'half a quarter-wave' isn't quite right. If ψ has a node at x=0 and an antinode at x=L, how many quarter-wavelengths fit exactly in the length L for the first mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but is still trying to get me to hand over key results. Their description of the first mode as 'half a quarter-wave' is a bit confused and worth addressing. I should gently correct their thinking and push them to figure out the k values themselves from the boundary conditions they already have.

exchanges.student: just say if it's λ/4, 3λ/4, 5λ/4 in L lol. i legit only need the final k_n/f_n forms.
exchanges.tutor: You're basically there — you just answered your own question! If those are the wavelengths that fit, can you write k_n in terms of n and L yourself? You know k = 2π/λ.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to the answer and is essentially stating it themselves. I should confirm their wavelength reasoning is correct without giving the k_n or f_n formulas directly, and push them to derive those themselves from what they already know.

exchanges.student: ok then can u just verify this is right: \(L=(2n-1)\lambda/4\), so \(k_n=(2n-1)\pi/(2L)\) and \(f_n=\sqrt{A}\,(2n-1)/(4L)\)?
exchanges.tutor: That's all correct! Now just plug in n=1,2,3 with L=1.5m and A=90000 m²/s² to get the three frequencies. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the correct expressions for k_n and f_n. I should confirm this is correct and nudge them to now apply it to get the actual numerical frequencies for the first 3 modes, which is what the problem asks for. This moves the conversation forward toward completing part (a).

exchanges.student: i get 50, 150, 250 Hz i think. can u just confirm those and also tell me the open-open length for same fundamental?
exchanges.tutor: Those frequencies are correct, good work! Now for the open-open pipe: what are the boundary conditions for ψ at both ends, and how does that change what fits in the pipe for the fundamental mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's frequencies are correct. I should confirm briefly and then ask them to reason about the open-open case themselves rather than giving them the answer. They need to think about what boundary conditions change and how that affects the fundamental wavelength.

exchanges.student: both ends antinodes for ψ, yeah. can u pls just give the length ratio closed-open vs open-open for same fundamental fr?
exchanges.tutor: You have everything you need! For the open-open fundamental, what fraction of λ fits in the pipe? Compare that to the closed-open case and set the frequencies equal — what length do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows the boundary conditions. Rather than giving the length ratio, I should push them to derive it themselves. They already found that for closed-open the fundamental has λ/4 fitting in L. Now they just need to figure out what fraction of λ fits in an open-open pipe for the fundamental, and set the frequencies equal. This is a short logical step they can do themselves.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the student presents a complete formula set (L=(2n-1)λ/4, k_n=(2n-1)π/(2L), f_n=√A·(2n-1)/(4L)) and the tutor confirms all of it as correct without any further probing. In Turn 9, the tutor confirms the three numerical frequencies (50, 150, 250 Hz) outright. Together these confirmations validate the full submission-ready answer for 6.1(a) and (c), effectively doing the final verification work for the student. However, the student did derive the formulas themselves, so this is borderline. The tutor's role was largely to confirm rather than generate, but the outright confirmation of all final numerical answers in Turn 9 without asking the student to check their own work crosses into providing near-submission-ready output.

The tutor never provides any method-level or process-level feedback throughout the conversation. When the student gets things right, the tutor simply confirms correctness (e.g., 'That's all correct!', 'Those frequencies are correct, good work!'). There is no feedback on the student's reasoning approach, how to think about boundary conditions systematically, or how to structure their solution — only correctness judgments.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently Socratic approach throughout, refusing to give direct answers and instead guiding the student through boundary conditions and mode structure via targeted questions. The student arrived at all key formulas and numerical answers through their own reasoning, with the tutor confirming correctness. The main weakness is the absence of any process-level or meta-learning feedback — the tutor only confirmed correctness without commenting on the student's reasoning method or approach quality. The 1.1.A.a deduction was considered but not applied because the student genuinely derived the formulas themselves; the tutor's confirmations, while direct, did not substitute for student reasoning.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor successfully avoided giving direct answers across all 10 turns, consistently redirecting the student to reason through boundary conditions and mode structure independently. The student derived the key formula L=(2n-1)λ/4 and the frequency expression themselves, with the tutor only confirming. No 1.1.A.a deduction was applied because the student carried the reasoning work. However, the tutor never offered any method-level feedback — all evaluative responses were simple correctness confirmations ('That's all correct!', 'Those frequencies are correct') without any commentary on the student's thinking process, approach quality, or how to generalize the method, triggering a full 1.3.A.b deduction. Dialogue quality and communication quality were strong: responses were concise, well-anchored to the assignment, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
